caption:
An average woman is exposed to 168 chemicals before breakfast.

They’re in your body wash, your lotion, your shampoo and they don’t just sit on your skin. Many of these chemicals are endocrine disruptors. They mimic your hormones, binding to the same receptors as estrogen, testosterone, and thyroid hormones.

Your body can’t always tell the difference between the real signal and the chemical copy.

The result? Disrupted cycles, sluggish thyroid, shifts in how your body processes glucose — effects most people never trace back to their skincare routine.

You can’t control every exposure. But you can measure what’s actually happening inside your body. Mito tracks over 100 biomarkers, including the ones most affected by environmental exposure.

caption:
You can be iron-deficient with perfect iron levels. Standard labs check iron. They skip the number that actually matters.

Ferritin is what tells you how much your body actually has in reserve. And most standard panels don’t test it. 

Low ferritin drives fatigue, hair loss, brain fog, and poor sleep long before your standard bloodwork raises a flag. And by the time iron “looks off”, you’ve often been depleted for months.

caption:
PCOS just got a new name. Here’s what it means for you.

After 11 years of global research and 22,000+ survey responses, experts officially renamed “polycystic ovary syndrome” to PMOS: Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome.

Why? Because the old name was wrong.
There are no abnormal cysts. The “polycystic” appearance on ultrasound is actually just follicles — completely normal ovarian activity. And the condition was never just an ovary problem. It involves insulin, androgens, neuroendocrine hormones, metabolism, and cardiovascular risk.

The new name reflects what the science has been saying for years: this is a whole-body, multi-hormone, metabolic condition. Not a cyst problem.

Here’s what each word means: 
→ Polyendocrine: multiple hormones are disrupted — not just estrogen and progesterone 
→ Metabolic: insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes risk, heart disease, and fatty liver are finally acknowledged 
→ Ovarian: broader than “cysts” — captures hormonal changes without implying something is structurally wrong

A better name means better awareness, faster diagnosis, and more complete care.

caption:
If you've been writing off bad sleep as just part of life, this one's worth reading.

Poor sleep raises inflammatory markers like hsCRP and IL-6, and that chronic inflammation is one of the main drivers of brain aging.

The good news? Inflammation is addressable.

Here are 6 habits, built around your biology, that lower it and protect your brain while you sleep.

Swipe to learn, then retest with Superpower to track your progress.
firstComment: Beauty sleep is a real thing 🧡😴

latestComments.text: Beauty sleep is a real thing 🧡😴

These are scammers! Dont waste your time and monies! Expensive. Oh and 14 tubes needed for their labs. Not 1! Deceitful advertising.

The accelerated aging mechanism is fascinating — chronic sleep debt elevates cortisol chronically, and cortisol directly breaks down collagen and elastin, the two proteins responsible for skin firmness and elasticity. On top of that, growth hormone — which is responsible for cellular repair and tissue regeneration — is released almost exclusively during slow-wave deep sleep. So every night of poor sleep is literally a night your body couldn’t repair itself properly. The compounding effect of that over months and years is what shows up on your face and in your biology.
Magnesium timing & dosage makes a significant difference — most people take it at the wrong time, and  wrong dose

All of this makes sense.
But most people don’t struggle with what to do, it’s just doing it consistently.

caption:
Most Americans have an Omega-3 Index below 4%.

That puts them in the highest risk category for cardiovascular disease and they don’t even know it.

Your Omega-3 Index measures the percentage of EPA and DHA in your red blood cell membranes. Below 4%, your inflammatory burden rises, cardiovascular risk doubles, and cognitive function takes a hit. Above 8%, you’re in the lowest risk category across the board. 

The fix isn’t complicated, and testing first can help guide smarter supplementation and bring your levels to optimal.
in your 30s/40s? you need to listen to this…

We’re the first generation to see diseases showing up earlier, and it’s not a coincidence. It’s because of the environment we grew up in.

Colorectal cancer is now rising fastest in adults under 50. But why?

Ultra-processed foods became the norm in the American diet.  Remote work, screens, and hours of sitting replaced our everyday movement. And preventive screening hasn’t kept pace with how fast risk is shifting.

So what can you actually do?
Start by understanding your baseline and get the data to know what’s happening inside your body from your inflammation, gut, heart, lipids, metabolism and more. Once you test your baseline, you’ll be able to make targeted changes in your lifestyle slowly that gradually improve your long-term health over time

caption: Mito Concierge pulls from your actual data and entire health history to provide personalized guidance based around your unique data and health concerns. Ask Concierge to adjust your supplements, flag what’s off in your labs, or any questions you may have about your health.

When workouts start feeling heavier and performance plateaus despite doing “everything right”, that’s not random.

Elevated hsCRP can signal ongoing inflammation.
Low ferritin can limit oxygen delivery to muscle.
Hormone shifts can affects strength, drive, and adaptation.

Sometimes poor recovery isn’t a motivation problem. It’s often a biological signal that most just aren’t measuring.

Your energy, brain fog, and “why am I so tired?” feeling isn’t always about taking B12. Sometimes it’s about taking the right form of B12.

Because B12 isn’t just one thing. It comes in four forms with different absorption, conversion needs, and best-use cases.

Save this for you next supplement aisle spiral.

Members can now export a PDF of their results directly from their dashboard, making it easy to bring to your next appointment or share with anyone involved in your care.

Cancer has long been considered a disease of aging. But that’s changed fast. Over the past two decades, rates among younger adults have been rising sharply. Learn the factors that may be putting you at risk and why early detection matters more than ever now.

~1 in 8 women have it. 
~70% may not know they have it. 

This is why the name change matters. 

PCOS didn't fully reflect the condition's complexity.

PMOS—Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome—is designed to better capture it.

If you've been told your symptoms are unrelated, or that your labs look normal, this is worth knowing.

Everyone’s doing it. Should you?

GLP-1s aren’t niche anymore. Nearly 12% of U.S. adults have tried one. And that number is about to skyrocket.

Why?

GLP-1s used to live in wellness clinics, Reddit threads, and the group chats of celebrities and the elites.

Now they’re spilling into everyday culture, fueled by a shift in how they’re used: microdosing.

Microdosing isn’t about rapid weight loss or extremes.

It’s about smaller, compounding benefits, without the heavy side effects:
• appetite control
• fewer mental negotiations with food
• steadier energy and blood sugar
• less inflammation
• cardiovascular health

This shift makes GLP-1s more sustainable, and far more desirable.

And when demand increases, supply follows.*

In five years, the question won’t be if you use GLP-1s, but how.

They won’t feel fringe. They’ll feel boring and familiar, as common as a probiotic or creatine.

_
* If you’re exploring GLP-1s, sourcing matters. Use only clinician-prescribed products from licensed, FDA-registered pharmacies with verified purity testing.

Logging miles is one thing. Optimizing them is another.  Today, our engineers clocked 13.1 miles at the SF Marathon — fueled by biomarker-backed training and recovery. 🧡  You can’t optimize endurance without understanding what’s happening under the hood. Biomarkers tied to oxygen delivery, inflammation, muscle damage, and stress are major players in how you feel and how you perform — yet they’re often overlooked.  
Case in point: Endurance athletes are especially prone to iron deficiency, and most don’t even know it. Even with solid nutrition, long miles can deplete iron and impact energy, stamina, and recovery.   Smarter training starts by decoding your biology.
